parents.mother_research_notes: Eunice Hollister Seymour (dau. of Capt. Stephen and Abigail Treat Hollister; b. 1696 Wethersfield CT)
Eunice Hollister Seymour (dau. of Capt. Stephen and Abigail Treat Hollister; b. 1696 Wethersfield CT)

notable.notable_blurb: Capt., CT Militia; innkeeper; 'Solomon Cowles and Sons'

narrative_blocks.header: Fined for drinking East India tea
narrative_blocks.body: On March 23, 1775, Solomon Cowles and his wife Martha were 'convicted of partaking of that detestable and obnoxious vegetable called East India tea' — forced to sign a public confession printed in the Connecticut Courant. Yet by 1774 Cowles had already served on the committee to collect provisions for the relief of Boston's inhabitants, and in 1775 receipted for supplies furnished to the Rifle Battalion marching to the American camp near Boston. He was a patriot despite the tea.

narrative_blocks.header: He rose to captain of the Farmington trainband
narrative_blocks.body: Cowles was commissioned an ensign in the Farmington trainband in 1759 and made captain in the colony's first regiment two years later. He also kept an inn on the town's main street and ran the firm Solomon Cowles and Sons.

parents.father_research_notes: Capt. Solomon Cowles of Farmington CT

notable.notable_blurb: Revolutionary lieutenant-colonel; Farmington farmer and tavern-keeper

military_service.pension: File No. 9153, U.S. Pension Office. Pensioned.
military_service.notes: Served 4th Sgt, 1st Co., Col. Hooker's 15th CT Militia, May 1776-May 1785. Jul 1776: went with company to New York under Capt. John Porter; Battle of Long Island; returned to Kingsbridge. Appointed quartermaster-sergeant by QM Jonathan Bull; served to close of the war. At Danbury Alarm; at New Haven (BTL018); at Battle of Saratoga (BTL011). Pensioned. Pension File No. 9153.

narrative_blocks.header: He fought from Long Island to Saratoga
narrative_blocks.body: Isaac Cowles rose from sergeant to lieutenant-colonel commandant of the 15th Connecticut Militia during the Revolution. He saw action at the Battle of Long Island, the Danbury and New Haven alarms, and Saratoga.

narrative_blocks.header: Afterward he kept a Farmington tavern
narrative_blocks.body: Back home in Farmington, Cowles farmed his father's homestead and kept a tavern, prospering into a man of local standing. A federal pension file recorded his long Revolutionary service.

cross_connections.display_label: was the father of her husband Isaac Cowles

narrative_blocks.body: In 1797, scarcely fifteen years old, Horace Cowles was admitted to the freshman class at Yale College. A growing disability forced him to leave in his senior year before examinations; invited by the faculty to attend with his class, he declined and was never enrolled as a graduate. He became a merchant with his brother, then justice of the peace, selectman, town clerk, state legislator, choir instructor, and member of the Missionary, Temperance, Anti-Masonic, and Anti-Slavery Societies.

narrative_blocks.header: One of three Mende girls from the Amistad
narrative_blocks.body: After the Amistad captives were freed, the Mende from that ship spent eight months in Farmington while arrangements were made for their return to Sierra Leone. Horace Cowles took in one of the three young Mende girls — believed to be Teme, also called Tamie — who attended school with the other Mende each morning and learned domestic skills with the Cowles children. Horace fell ill toward the end of the Mende's stay and died in 1841; his son Samuel Smith inherited the house and continued the Underground Railroad work.

open_questions.question: Is Rev. Noah Porter Sr. (1781-1866), who preached Horace Cowles's funeral sermon, a relation to the Hooker tree?
open_questions.notes: Noah Porter Sr. was minister of the First Church of Christ in Farmington 1806-1866 (60 years). He preached Horace Cowles's funeral sermon, published as a 26-page pamphlet. His son Noah Porter Jr. (1811-1892) became president of Yale. The Porter family is prominent in Farmington history — worth checking whether there is a Hooker or Cowles family connection. (Cowles Genealogy #642)

cross_connections.display_label: Rev. Noah Porter preached his funeral sermon — 26-page pamphlet, Farmington 1841
Willcox held the Mende captives in federal custody during their trial; Cowles housed one of the Mende girls in his Farmington home after the Supreme Court freed them in 1841.
Both Cowles cousins sheltered Mende girls in 1841
cross_connections.description: When the freed Amistad Mende were brought to Farmington in 1841, Horace Cowles and his cousin Major Timothy Cowles each took in one of the three surviving Mende girls. Horace sheltered Teme; Timothy is believed to have sheltered Kagne.

Fellow Hooker-descendant Connecticut abolitionist of the same generation.

education.institution_blurb: Admitted 1797 age 15; left in senior year due to disability; never formally graduated despite faculty invitation.

sources.short: Edward Hooker 1909 — entry #698 Major Timothy Cowles

notable.notable_blurb: Wealthy Farmington merchant who sheltered a Mende child

narrative_blocks.header: Built Farmington's stone store — and ran it
narrative_blocks.body: Major Timothy Cowles was a wealthy Farmington merchant whose home sat just south of the Congregational Church. He built the stone store destroyed by fire in 1864, owned the large hotel building later occupied by Porter Seminary, and served as state senator (1835), member of the Yale Corporation (1835) and liberal contributor to Yale's $100,000 fund, and president of Farmington Bank (1852). His estate at death was inventoried at $147,634.

When the three Mende girls who had survived the 1839 Amistad rebellion were brought to Farmington in 1841, the Cowles family took in one of them — believed by NPS to be Kagne. She would have attended school five hours each morning and shared in the family's domestic routines.

narrative_blocks.header: Gave liberally to Yale's Hundred Thousand Fund
narrative_blocks.body: Timothy Cowles graduated Yale and later served as a 'liberal contributor' to the Hundred Thousand Dollar Fund raised by Yale alumni in support of the college. He earned the title 'Major Timothy' from Connecticut militia service that followed him into family records.

institutions.institution_blurb: Member of the Yale Corporation 1835; contributed to Yale's $100,000 fund.

cross_connections.display_label: Both Cowles cousins sheltered Mende girls in 1841
cross_connections.description: When the freed Amistad Mende were brought to Farmington in 1841, Timothy Cowles and his cousin Horace Cowles each took in one of the three surviving Mende girls. Timothy is believed to have sheltered Kagne; Horace sheltered Teme.

cross_connections.display_label: Both Farmington households that sheltered Mende girls in 1841
cross_connections.description: During the Amistad survivors' 1841 Farmington stay, Rev. Noah Porter Jr.'s family took in Margru while Timothy Cowles sheltered the girl believed to be Kagne. Both families were anchors of the Farmington abolitionist network that received the freed Mende.

cross_connections.display_label: Williams brought the Mende to Farmington — Cowles took one in
cross_connections.description: Austin Williams was one of the Farmington abolitionists who organized the freed Mende's relocation to Farmington after the 1841 Supreme Court ruling. Timothy Cowles was among the families who answered Williams's call by sheltering a Mende child.

cross_connections.display_label: Held the Mende during trial — Cowles housed one after release
cross_connections.description: Norris Willcox held the Mende captives in federal custody during their trial. After the Supreme Court freed them in March 1841, the Mende were brought to Farmington, where Major Timothy Cowles was among the families who sheltered one of the freed Mende girls.

cross_connections.display_label: Married his fourth cousin — both descend Rev. Samuel Hooker.

military_service.notes: Quartermaster 1808-9; ensign 1809; lt. Grenadier co. 1811; captain 1812-14; major 1815; major 14th CT Militia 1816-17.
Got the title 'Major Timothy' through service in the Connecticut militia. The title stuck in family records and EH 1909.

career.notes: Liberal contributor to the $100,000 fund for the assistance of the college.

career.title: Merchant and farmer
career.notes: Merchant and farmer at Farmington CT per EH #698.

career.title: Connecticut State Senator
career.notes: Served as State Senator per EH #698.

education.notes: Yale graduate per EH #698. Specific year not documented in EH text.

military_service.notes: Lt. 15th CT Militia 1815; lt. 14th CT Militia 1816-17; ensign 1817-18; lt. 1819-20; captain 1821-24.

narrative_blocks.body: Solomon died in 1795 at the age of one. Solomon’s parents gave the name to a child born one year later.

narrative_blocks.header: His homestead stood on the site of Rev.
narrative_blocks.body: Solomon Cowles lived on his father's homestead in Farmington — the house built upon the site of the original parsonage occupied by Rev. Samuel Hooker when he was minister at Farmington. He was a farmer and manufacturer of soap and candles, joined the church in 1834. His wife Mary Emeline Sloper was buried beside him at Farmington.

cross_connections.display_label: His homestead stood on the site of Rev. Samuel Hooker's original parsonage

naming_inspiration.description: Named 'Samuel Hooker' to honor the Hooker family lineage through his mother Lucina Hooker Cowles (H00309) and the family's descent from Rev. Thomas Hooker.

institution_affiliations.institution_blurb: Graduated Andover Theological Seminary 1824 but never preached.

education.institution_blurb: Graduated M.A. Yale 1821. See Biographical Notices of Yale College Graduates.

parents.mother_research_notes: Polly Gleason Cowles of Farmington CT (not built per Jalapeño). Wife of Capt. Solomon Cowles X01975.

cross_connections.display_label: Married her first cousin Alfred from the Hooker-line Cowles branch.

notable.notable_blurb: Illinois lawyer who later migrated to California

cross_connections.display_label: Married his first cousin Charlotte from the parallel Cowles line.

career.title: Lawyer
career.notes: Practiced law in Illinois (Belleville per Cowles book — three children born there).

career.title: Lawyer
career.notes: Lawyer of note. Practiced first in Belleville Illinois (where his three eldest children were born 1817-1825), then migrated to California by 1884. Still practicing in San Diego at age 97 per Egbert's 1884 sketch.

narrative_blocks.header: Practicing law in San Diego at ninety-seven
narrative_blocks.body: Alfred Cowles studied law and migrated to Belleville Illinois with his cousin-wife Charlotte where their three eldest children were born 1817-1825. By 1884 he had moved to San Diego California and was still practicing 'as a lawyer of note' at age 97. His elder brother Egbert's 1884 biographical sketch records the fact, but Alfred's death year remains unconfirmed.

narrative_blocks.header: The books never recorded his death
narrative_blocks.body: In September 1816 he married his first cousin Charlotte Cowles; the family lawyered in Belleville, Illinois, then followed two daughters to California. Neither the Hooker genealogy nor the Cowles book ever learned when he died — last seen “a lawyer of note” in San Diego, past ninety-seven.

naming_inspiration.description: Named for his uncle Frederick Woodruff Cowles (H01140) who died at 18 months before this Frederick was born.

burial.plot_notes: Mount Hope Cemetery San Diego CA per Cowles book #1695. CEM not built — single Hooker connection.

education.notes: Educated at Willoughby Female Seminary, Lake County Ohio.

cross_connections.display_label: Pioneered California while her grandfather served the Revolution.
Distant Hooker cousins, both touched by the Civil War.

narrative_blocks.header: Belleville Illinois to Ensenada Baja California
narrative_blocks.body: Cornelia was born in Belleville Illinois in 1829, where her father Alfred practiced law. Her family migrated to California in 1853, and she married Mexican-War-era medical officer Erasmus Darwin French in San Jose January 1858. By 1894 the couple resided in Ensenada Baja California Mexico — three thousand miles from her father's Connecticut birthplace.

education.notes: Educated at Willoughby Female Seminary, Lake County Ohio.

naming_inspiration.description: Middle name 'Gleason' for her maternal grandmother Polly Gleason Cowles.

narrative_blocks.header: Willoughby Seminary then a Nevada City marriage
narrative_blocks.body: Elizabeth was born 1833 in Belleville Illinois and educated at Willoughby Female Seminary in Lake County Ohio (the same school as her elder sister Cornelia). She married enterprising Nevada City California merchant Charles Carroll Leavitt at her parents' San Jose home; the family later settled in Oakland while Leavitt served in the San Francisco Custom House for many years.

naming_inspiration.description: Named for both father (Alfred Cowles H01139) and paternal grandfather (Ezekiel Cowles Jr. H00439).

burialCemetery.name: Memento Mori Cemetery
burialCemetery.notes: Rev. Samuel Hooker buried here; no stone survives.
